note:
在造成两人死亡的枪击事件之前，民兵的存在引发了抗议者的批评，警方也给予了一些支持。
亚当·马奥尼在基诺沙，露易丝·贝克特和朱莉娅·凯莉·王在奥克兰，维多利亚·贝肯皮斯在纽约。
在威斯康星州基诺沙发生警察枪击事件的抗议活动中，一名17岁的白人男子据称杀害了两人，打伤了第三人。几个小时前，当地一个民兵组织在Facebook上发帖称：“今晚有爱国者愿意拿起武器保卫我们的城市不受邪恶暴徒的伤害吗？”
目前尚不清楚昨晚基诺沙枪击案后因涉嫌谋杀而被捕的伊利诺伊州少年凯尔·里滕豪斯(Kyle Rittenhouse)是否在响应该民兵组织发布的电话的人之列，该电话后来出现在阴谋论网站InfoWars上。
Rittenhouse名下列出的一个Facebook账户包括这名年轻白人男子用军用步枪发帖的图片，以及多个表达支持执法的帖子。
他在个人资料照片上加入了支持警察的标语“蓝色生命也是命”。2018年的一篇帖子要求人们向人性化徽章捐款，这是一个非营利性组织，寻求在警察和社区之间建立“更牢固的”关系。
Facebook表示，尚未找到证据表明里滕豪斯关注了民兵组织基诺沙卫队(Kenosha Guard)的页面，也没有证据表明他受到了他们为周二组织的活动页面的邀请，该页面敦促武装公民保护自己的生命和财产。
但它在周三证实，它已经删除了基诺沙警卫队的页面，因为它违反了针对与暴力有关的组织和运动的新政策，包括民兵组织。
周三上午，该组织在Facebook上发帖称，他们“不知道”枪手是否“响应了基诺沙卫队民兵的武装号召”。Facebook将该组织拿下前不久，该组织就在Facebook上发帖称，他们“不知道”枪手是否“响应”了基诺沙卫队民兵的武装号召。
该组织的创始人、36岁的凯文·马修森(Kevin Mathewson)表示，一名17岁的少年“无论在法律上还是在道德上都没有资格携带武器”，但枪手“要对自己的行为负责”。
在枪击事件发生之前，周二晚上，武装的白人男子出现在基诺沙的黑人生命也是抗议活动中，招致了抗议者的批评-以及执法部门的一些支持。
在周二晚间广泛流传的一段视频中，可以听到基诺沙警方从一辆装甲车上向走在街上的似乎是武装平民表示感谢，并向他们扔瓶装水的声音。
“感谢您的光临，”一名警官通过扩音器说。
但县警长大卫·贝斯特(David Best)在枪击事件发生后告诉媒体，在基诺沙街头巡逻的人“就像一个义务警员团体”，并表示，枪击事件证明了为什么建议代理公民以帮助执法部门遏制该市的骚乱是一个糟糕的想法。
“地狱没有…。不行。昨晚是我不会这么做的完美理由，“他周三下午在基诺沙的记者会上说。
贝斯特说，他在社交媒体上看到的民兵通知“看起来像派对邀请函”。
他说，委托公民提供执法将意味着这些人将受到治安官办公室的保护。
“所以这实际上应该是昨晚杀害两人的副警长。随之而来的责任将是巨大的，“他说。
威斯康星州民兵组织基诺沙警卫队(Kenosha Guard)周二晚间呼吁武装公民在基诺沙巡逻。该组织的创始人、前当地民选官员告诉英国“卫报”(The Guardian)，该组织规模较小，最近才成立，与其说是一个有组织的组织，不如说是一个“普遍的战斗号召”。
36岁的前基诺沙市议员马修森(Mathewson)以与市政府官员发生冲突而闻名，他说，在警察枪杀雅各布·布莱克(Jacob Blake)后，他对该市的动乱感到不安。
他说，该组织的信息是呼吁美国人行使携带武器的权利：“如果你热爱我们的城市，不想看到它被烧毁，那就拿起你的枪，走到外面去。”
马修森说，阴谋论网站InfoWars报道了基诺沙警卫队在Facebook上的帖子和计划于周二晚间举行的活动，引发了热烈的反响，数百人发来了私信，数千人表示可能会参加活动。
但一些看到武装人员的抗议者，包括马修森(Mathewson)，批评了他们的存在。马修森说，他在周二晚上携带了一支AR-15步枪。
布拉德福德社区教堂的莫妮卡·卡明斯牧师告诉芝加哥哥伦比亚广播公司，“我质疑那位身着军装、手持武器出现在和平抗议活动中的绅士。”
在民兵的Facebook帖子进入社区后，一些抗议者表示，他们担心“白人至上主义暴力”会破坏他们原本和平的抗议活动。
“我们在Facebook上看到白人至上主义者威胁要枪杀我们所有人，搞砸我们的运动，”抗议者克里斯·罗斯曼(Chris Rosmann)告诉“卫报”，他站在雅各布·布莱克被枪击的地方附近的一个公园里，与不同的示威者聚集在一起。
他补充说：“我们知道媒体会将叙事转向对他们有利，尽管造成伤害的不是我们。”
一家烧毁的汽车经销商是骚乱的商业受害者之一，在一群站在外面的男子中，一位名叫乔什(Josh)的当地人表示：“当地人肯定不完全同意这里的警方，但我们都对进来煽动暴力的人感到困惑和害怕。”出于安全原因，他要求不透露自己的姓氏。
他说，他一生都住在基诺沙，对正在发生的暴力事件感到困惑。
一些旁观者说，周二晚上的局势非常混乱，就连现场的一些抗议者和媒体都不清楚枪声是来自民兵类型的义务警员，还是一些当地的企业主拿着枪在他们的财产周围站岗。
Facebook于8月19日宣布了对与暴力有关的团体采取行动的新政策，旨在解决那些“显示出对公共安全构成重大风险”但没有上升到恐怖组织或仇恨团体级别的团体。在宣布这项政策后，Facebook表示，它已经禁止了980个组和520个页面，这些组和520个页面与美国的民兵或组织有关，这些组织“认为自己是安提法”。
note_en:
Prior to shooting that left two dead, presence of militia prompted criticism from protesters – and some support from police
   Adam Mahoney in Kenosha,  Lois Beckett and  Julia Carrie Wong in Oakland and  Victoria Bekiempis in New York
 Hours before a 17-year-old white man allegedly killed two people and injured a third at protests over a police shooting in Kenosha,  Wisconsin, a local militia group posted a call on Facebook: “Any patriots willing to take up arms and defend our city tonight from evil thugs?”
 It’s not yet clear if Kyle Rittenhouse, the Illinois teenager arrested on suspicion of murder after last night’s shootings in Kenosha, was among the people who responded to the call the militia group had posted, which was later featured on the conspiracy theory site InfoWars.
  A  Facebook account listed under Rittenhouse’s name included images of the young white man posting with a military-style rifle, as well as multiple posts expressing support for law enforcement.
  He included the pro-police slogan “blue lives matter” on his profile photo. A post in 2018 asked for people to donate to Humanizing the Badge, a non-profit organization that seeks to create “stronger” relationships between police and communities.
 Facebook said that it had not yet found evidence that suggested that Rittenhouse had followed the page of the militia group, Kenosha Guard, or that he was invited on the event page they organized for Tuesday, which urged armed citizens to protect their lives and property.
 But it confirmed on Wednesday that it had taken down Kenosha Guard’s page for violating its new policy against organizations and movements tied to violence, including militia groups.
 Kenosha Guard said that it was “unaware” if the shooter was “answering the Kenosha Guard Militia’s call to arms”, the group posted on Facebook on Wednesday morning, shortly before Facebook took it down.
 The group’s founder, 36-year-old Kevin Mathewson, said that a 17-year-old “had no business being armed legally or ethically”, but the shooter is “responsible for his own actions”.
 Before the shooting, the presence of armed white men at the Black Lives Matter protests in Kenosha on Tuesday night prompted criticism from protesters – and some support from law enforcement.
 In a widely circulating video from Tuesday night, Kenosha police can be heard thanking and tossing bottled water from an armored vehicle to what appear to be armed civilians walking the streets.
 “We appreciate you being here,” an officer is heard saying over a loudspeaker.
 But David Best, the county sheriff, told media outlets after the shooting that people patrolling the streets in Kenosha were a “like a vigilante group”, and said that the shooting was evidence of why suggestions to deputize citizens to help law enforcement contain the unrest in the city had been a bad idea.
  “Hell no … no way. Last night was the perfect reason why I would not do that,” he said at a press conference in Kenosha on Wednesday afternoon.
 Best said that militia notices he saw on social media “looked like party invitations”.
 He said that deputizing citizens to provide law enforcement would entail such individuals coming under the umbrella of the sheriff’s office.
 “So that would in reality have been a deputy sheriff who killed two people last night. The liability that goes with that would have been immense,” he said.
 Kenosha Guard, the Wisconsin militia group that called for armed citizens to patrol Kenosha on Tuesday night, was small, recently formed and less a structured organization than a “general call to arms”, its founder, a former local elected official, told the Guardian.
 Mathewson, a 36-year former Kenosha city alderman known for  clashing with city officials, said he was upset at the unrest in the city after the police shooting of Jacob Blake.
 The group’s message, he said, was a call to Americans to exercise their right to bear arms: “If you love our city and don’t want to see it burn, get your guns and go outside.”
 Kenosha Guard’s Facebook posts and planned Tuesday night event were covered by the conspiracy theory website InfoWars, and prompted a passionate response, Mathewson said, with private messages from hundreds of people, and thousands of people who indicated they might attend the event.
 But some protesters who saw the armed men, including Mathewson, who said he was carrying an AR-15 rifle on Tuesday night, criticized their presence.
 “I question that gentleman showing up in military fatigues and sidearms at a peaceful protest,” the Rev Dr Monica Cummings of Bradford Community church told CBS Chicago.
 After the militia’s Facebook posts made their way to the community, some protesters said they feared that “white supremacist violence” would derail their otherwise peaceful protests.
 “We saw white supremacists on Facebook threatening to gun us all down and mess up our movement,” Chris Rosmann, a protester standing in a park near where  Jacob Blake was shot, gathering with a diverse group of demonstrators, told the Guardian.
  “And we know the media is going to shift the narrative in their favor, even though that’s not us causing the harm,” he added.
 Among a group of men standing outside a burnt-out car dealership, one of the business casualties of the unrest, a local named Josh, who asked to withhold his last name for safety reasons, said: “Locals definitely are not in total agreement with the police here, but we’re all just confused and scared by the people coming in and stoking violence,” he said.
 He said he had lived in Kenosha all his life and was confused by the violence taking place.
 Some bystanders said that the situation on Tuesday night was so chaotic that it was unclear even to some protesters and media on the scene whether gunfire was coming only from militia-type vigilantes or also some local business owners standing guard with guns over their properties.
 Facebook’s new policy to take action on groups linked to violence was  announced on 19 August and was designed to address groups that “demonstrated significant risks to public safety” but did not rise to the level of terrorist organizations or hate groups. Upon announcement of the policy, Facebook said it had banned 980 groups and 520 pages associated with US-based militias or groups that “identify as Antifa”.
title: 武装白人男子在Facebook上组织的基诺沙抗议活动中巡逻
title_en: Armed white men patrolling Kenosha protests organized on Facebook

note:
又一次提醒人们，2020年是一个超现实的地狱般的狂热梦想，我们注定要永远被困在里面。看起来，格莱美获得者、运动鞋鉴赏家和政治失败者坎耶·韦斯特(Kanye West)正因涉嫌窃取商业技术机密这一硅谷久经考验的策略而被起诉。
首先被TMZ发现的是总部位于宾夕法尼亚州的小型电商公司MyChannel(简称MYC)。MYC声称，在投入数百万美元和半年的时间为叶诗文的在线服装店模拟一个漂亮的新网站后，这位说唱歌手退出了他们的合同。根据诉讼，韦斯特随后将公司的想法据为己有，从事情的声音来看，只是...。他们被蒙蔽了-违背了多个承诺，违反了NDA，并在这个过程中充当了一个巨大的工具。
但这还不是问题的一半。正如孩子们会说的那样，MYC整整30多页的控诉-你可以在本文的底部读到-是相当令人畏惧的。根据日程安排，韦斯特最初与MYC签约是在2018年春天，承诺如果该公司为他的电商网站创建一个功能强大的视频平台，他不仅会为其服务向该公司支付费用，还会向该业务投资巨额1000万美元。MYC还让West签署了一份保密协议，以确保该公司的专有视频技术不会在没有支付任何费用的情况下被“敲竹杠”。
可能假设坎耶会信守诺言，MYC表示，他们的团队在接下来的六个月里在这个项目上每周工作80个小时，在这个过程中花费了数万美元开发拟议的视频软件。不仅如此，由于坎耶“要求”团队将总部从费城搬到加州，后来又搬到芝加哥，生活费用让他们陷入了更深的困境。总而言之，MYC声称在与韦斯特对峙并告诉他履行协议之前，已经花费了700万美元的自有资金。
这可能是一个很好的时机来提一下，虽然MYC似乎已经摆脱了它的网站，但该公司在投资中心Crunchbase上的页面指出，到目前为止，它收到了大约140万美元的投资-而且只列出了三名在该公司工作的员工。
这起诉讼没有履行他那一边的协议，而是描述了韦斯特-值得指出的是一位字面上的亿万富翁-是如何想出一些“不真实的、被认为是轻视的”，并切断了与MYC团队的所有联系，让他们陷入困境，负债累累。与此同时，韦斯特在随后的几个月里立即使用了MYC所说的他们平台的近乎碳的副本，作为“星期日礼拜”的推广活动的一部分，这是韦斯特所谓的弹出式教堂体验。
一个以假福音为主题的电子商务平台是一个愚蠢的想法，但它也是一个非常、非常有利可图的想法。早在3月份，坎耶就在他的Instagram账户上吹嘘说，这些活动带来的电商销售让他每晚赚了整整100万美元-或者说，仅从被盗软件一年就赚了3.5亿美元。
MYC可能比我们看到的由谷歌(Google)等公司高调提起的破坏NDA的案件规模更小，而坎耶不太可能面临与安东尼·莱万多夫斯基(Anthony Levandowski)在加州法院认定他典当该公司专有技术罪名成立时受到的同样愤怒或监禁的任何事情。韦斯特也没有被指控像亚马逊那样敲诈小型科技公司。
这在很大程度上可能要归功于这样一个事实，即职业音乐家坎耶·韦斯特(Kanye West)并不是一个大部分靠科技产品谋生的名字。但这起案件仍然带有同样的商业恶作剧的味道，每当有钱有权的人利用没有钱和权力的人时，这种恶作剧似乎总是会发生。
note_en:
In yet another reminder of how 2020 is a surreal, hellish fever dream that we’re doomed to be trapped inside forever, it looks like Kanye West—Grammy   winner, sneaker   connoisseur, and political   underdog—is being sued for pulling the tried-and-true Silicon Valley tactic of allegedly   stealing trade tech secrets.
    First   spotted by TMZ, the suit is being spearheaded by small, Pennsylvania-based ecommerce company MyChannel (MYC, for short). MYC allege that after pouring millions of dollars and half a year’s worth of work into mocking up a spiffy new site for Ye’s online clothing store, the rapper stepped out on their contract. According to the lawsuit, West then took the company’s ideas for himself, and from the sound of things, just... ghosted them—breaking multiple promises, violating NDAs, and acting like a huge tool in the process.
 But that’s not the half of it. The full 30+ pages of MYC’s complaint—which you can read at the bottom of this article—are, as the kids would  say, pretty cringe. According to the docket, West initially contracted MYC back in the spring of 2018 with the promise that if the company created a juiced-up video platform for his e-commerce site, he’d not only, y’know, pay the company for its services, but would invest a hefty $10 million into the business. MYC also had West sign an NDA just to make sure that the company’s proprietary video tech wouldn’t be “ripped off” without any payment.
  Probably assuming that Kanye would keep his word, MYC says its team spent the next six months clocking 80 hour workweeks on the project, spending tens of thousands on the proposed video software in the process. Not only that, but because Kanye “demanded” that the team move its HQ from its home in Philly over to California, and later Chicago, living expenses sunk them even deeper into the hole. All told, MYC claims to have spent spent $7 million of its own funds before confronting West and telling him to make good on his end of the deal.
 This might be a good time to mention that while MYC seems to have gotten rid of its website, the   company’s page on the investment hub Crunchbase notes that to date it received around $1.4 million in investments—and only lists three staffers working at the company at all.
   Instead of fulfilling his side of the bargain, the suit describes how West—who it’s worth pointing out is a literal   billionaire—came up with some “untrue perceived slight,” and cut all ties with MYC’s team, leaving them stranded and in a mountain of debt. Meanwhile, West spent the months immediately afterward using what MYC describes as a near-carbon copy of their platform as part of the promotion for “Sunday Service,” West’s so-called pop-up church   experience.
 A faux-gospel-themed-ecommerce-platform is a batshit idea, but it’s also one that’s been very, very profitable.  Back in March, Kanye bragged on his Instagram account that the ecommerce sales off the backs of these events were netting him a good one million dollars per night—or, a good $350 million annually from the pilfered software alone.
  MYC might be smaller potatoes than the high-profile NDA-busting cases we’ve seen brought to court by the likes of say,   Google, and Kanye is unlikely to face anything close to the same wrath—or prison sentence—that Anthony Levandowski was struck with when the California courts found him   guilty of pawning off that company’s proprietary tech. Nor is West being accused of   ripping off small-time tech firms on the scale of Amazon.
 Much of that distinction is likely owed to the fact that Kanye West, a musician by trade, isn’t a name that makes most of his living through tech products. But the case still smacks of the same business-side shenanigans that always seems to happen whenever someone with money and power takes advantage of people who don’t.
title: 坎耶·韦斯特被控掠夺商业秘密技术为其互联网教会提供资金
title_en: Kanye West Accused of Plundering Trade-Secret Tech To Fund His Internet Church
